title: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme
focus: Coverage criteria, integrated schemes, and compensation powers of the RBI Ombudsman.
rationale: Highly testable due to specific coverage thresholds and compensation limits.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme covers Commercial Banks, UCBs with deposits of 50 crore, NBFCs with assets of 100 crore, System Participants, and Credit Information Companies. It integrates the Banking, NBFC, and Digital Transactions Ombudsman schemes under a One Nation One Ombudsman approach. The Ombudsman can award up to 20 lakh in compensation and 1 lakh for distress.
evidenceBullets: Covers UCBs with deposits of 50 crore and NBFCs with assets of 100 crore.
Integrates Banking (2006), NBFC (2018), and Digital Transactions (2019) Ombudsman schemes.
Ombudsman can award up to 20 lakh in compensation and 1 lakh for mental distress.

title: RBI Liquidity and Debt Management Tools
focus: Mechanisms of Standing Deposit Facility (SDF), Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR), and Switch Auctions.
rationale: Core monetary policy tools; SDF and VRRR are critical for liquidity management and frequently tested in UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: Standing Deposit Facility (SDF) absorbs liquidity without collateral, replacing the Fixed Rate Reverse Repo as the LAF corridor floor. It is available every day. Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR) absorbs excess money for short periods at market-decided rates. Switch Auctions manage public debt maturity by replacing short-term securities with longer-dated ones via the e-Kuber portal.
evidenceBullets: SDF absorbs liquidity from commercial banks without collateral and replaced the Fixed Rate Reverse Repo as the LAF floor.
SDF is available every day, including holidays and weekends.
VRRR interest rates are decided by the market, unlike the fixed Reverse Repo Rate.
Switch Auctions replace short-term government securities with longer-dated ones to manage debt maturity.

title: Co-lending Norms and Payment Aggregators
focus: Regulatory guidelines for Co-Lending Arrangements (CLA) and Payment Aggregators.
rationale: Important regulatory updates for NBFCs, banks, and fintech entities.
sectionExcerpt: Under Co-Lending Arrangements between banks and NBFCs, each Regulated Entity must retain a minimum 10 percent share of the loans. Lenders can claim Priority Sector Lending status for their share. Uniform asset classification applies; if one lender tags a loan as NPA, others must follow. Payment Aggregators facilitate merchant payments, are incorporated under the Companies Act 2013, and non-bank PAs require RBI authorisation under the PSS Act 2007.
evidenceBullets: Co-lending norms require each Regulated Entity to retain a minimum 10 percent share of the loans.
Co-lenders must uniformly classify assets; an NPA tag by one requires the same by others.
Non-bank Payment Aggregators must obtain RBI authorisation under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.

title: Ways and Means Advances (WMA)
focus: Purpose, limits, interest rates, and duration of WMAs for central and state governments.
rationale: WMA is a standard fiscal-monetary interface topic with clear factual parameters.
sectionExcerpt: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) are temporary advances by RBI to central and state governments to cover receipt-payment mismatches, not to finance fiscal deficits. Authorized by the RBI Act 1934, the interest rate is the repo rate, while overdrafts are charged 2 percent above the repo rate. WMAs must be fully paid off within 3 months.
evidenceBullets: WMA is a temporary advance for receipt-payment mismatches and does not finance the fiscal deficit.
Available to both the Central government and state governments/Union Territories.
Interest rate on WMA is the RBI repo rate, and overdrafts are 2 percent above the repo rate.
WMA must be fully paid off within 3 months.

title: RBI Digital Initiatives and Certificate of Deposits
focus: Unified Lending Interface, CBDC sandbox, and characteristics of Certificate of Deposits.
rationale: Emerging digital infrastructure and traditional money market instruments (CDs) are prime targets for prelims questions.
sectionExcerpt: RBI initiatives include the Unified Lending Interface (ULI), a Digital Public Infrastructure for credit delivery, and a CBDC retail sandbox for fintechs. Tokenisation of Certificate of Deposits (CDs) is also introduced. CDs are negotiable money market instruments issued at a discount to face value by Scheduled Commercial Banks (excluding RRBs and LABs) and select FIs, with a maturity of 7 days to one year.
evidenceBullets: Unified Lending Interface (ULI) is a Digital Public Infrastructure aiming to enhance credit delivery.
Certificate of Deposits (CDs) are issued by Scheduled Commercial Banks, excluding Regional Rural Banks and Local Area Banks.
CDs are issued at a discount on face value with a maturity period ranging from 7 days to one year.

title: Self-Regulatory Organisation for NBFCs
focus: Recognition of FIDC and eligibility criteria for SROs under RBI framework.
rationale: Small but specific regulatory development regarding NBFC oversight.
sectionExcerpt: RBI granted Self-Regulatory Organisation (SRO) status to the Finance Industry Development Council (FIDC) to oversee NBFCs. Backed by RBI's Omnibus Framework 2024, an eligible SRO must be set up as a not-for-profit company registered under Section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013.
evidenceBullets: RBI granted SRO status to the Finance Industry Development Council (FIDC) to oversee NBFCs.
An SRO must be established as a not-for-profit company under Section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013.
